entry.title: Getting ready for SAP Integration Suite at SAP Sapphire 2026!
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite - Sapphire 2026

 Hi Community, 

Excited to dive into what’s coming next for integration, AI, and real-time enterprise architecture at SAP Sapphire 2026. This year’s sessions clearly show how integration is evolving from a backend capability into a strategic enabler for AI-driven business. 

Don’t miss the opportunity to gain insights, learn from customer success stories, and experience how integration is becoming the backbone for AI innovation.  

Join us at SAP Sapphire 2026! SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference Orlando | SAP Sapphire Virtual | SAP Sapphire Madrid 

Save the dates and register today.  

🚨 Stay tuned for session highlights and updates throughout the event! Explore Integration Suite Catalogue here! 

SAP Integration Suite empowers organizations to connect applications, data, and processes across hybrid landscapes while enabling AI-driven innovation. From prebuilt integrations to event-driven architectures and governance for AI agents, this track highlights how integration is evolving into a strategic enabler for intelligent enterprises. Take a look at our SAP Integration Suite sessions and demos👇 

SAP Integration Suite at Sapphire 2026: Product Sessions

In these sessions, leading organizations share how they are leveraging SAP Integration Suite to solve real-world challenges. From enabling real-time commerce to scaling event-driven architectures and building AI-ready integration foundations, these stories provide practical insights into how integration drives business transformation across industries. 

BTP1445: Accelerate integrations with large volumes of adapters and prebuilt content 

BTP1446: Why organizations consolidate integration tools to prepare for AI 

BTP1447: Enterprise-ready AI integration: Securely connect and govern your AI agents 

BTP2100: Modernize from SAP Process Integration and other integration platforms 

SAP Integration Suite at Sapphire 2026: Customer Sessions

Leading organizations will share how they are leveraging SAP Integration Suite to solve real-world challenges. From enabling real-time commerce to scaling event-driven architectures and building AI-ready integration foundations, these stories provide practical insights into how integration drives business transformation across industries: 

BTP2350: FC Bayern Munich - Connecting fan data and systems with SAP Integration Suite 

BTP2138: Under Armour - Enabling real-time commerce with SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) 

BTP2103: Siemens: Scaling real-time integration with event-driven design solutions 

BTP2637: Florida Crystals - Modernize B2B integration with SAP Integration Suite at scale 

BTP2638: PPG Industries - Building an AI-ready, clean core integration foundation

SAP Integration Suite at Sapphire 2026: Demo Stations

The demo stations offer a hands-on look at how SAP Integration Suite capabilities come to life in practice. These sessions will showcase how to modernize integrations for AI and implement governance for AI-driven scenarios, giving a closer view of tools and approaches that can be directly applied in enterprise landscapes. 

BTP2137: Modernize Integrations for AI 

BTP2138: Govern AI-Driven Integrations 

SAP Integration Suite at Sapphire 2026: ASUG Preer Insights

This session highlights the SAP Customer Influence site as a transparent, AI-powered platform for submitting, tracking, and prioritizing ideas that shape the future of SAP Integration Suite. It enables customers to share real-time feedback, influence product roadmaps, and gain visibility into how other organizations are driving integration-led transformation across their landscapes. 

BTP1606: Customer Influence and feedback: Influence SAP Integration Suite 

 BTP1359: Deep dive into SAP Integration Suite: AI and event-driven integration

BTP1360: Modernize your strategy: Migrating from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite

BTP1361. The road ahead: Agentic AI and strategy with SAP Integration Suite

Explore these sessions and plan your agenda to get the most out of SAP Sapphire 2026. Don’t miss out!

entry.title: Integration capabilities highly rated in SAP BTP reviews
entry.content:
Only a few short months ago I posted a blog in celebration of SAP Business Technology Platform's (SAP BTP) performance in G2’s Winter 2026 awards for their Cloud Platform as a Service (PaaS) category. Now having quickly accelerated to their Spring 2026 awards, once again I am happy to report that SAP BTP featured prominently, achieving leader badges along with a leaders position in the G2 Enterprise Grid for CPaaS.  

 Connections count!

Reading just a handful of the most recent SAP BTP reviews on the G2 website reveals just how important robust yet flexible integration is to SAP customers. Among the most recent customer reviews, many reveal Integration as something for which they rate SAP BTP highly:

  • 18 April 2026 I use SAP Business Technology Platform for data integration, analytics, and app development. It connects multiple systems, reduces data silos, and streamlines workflows, providing real-time insights. Its scalability handles large data efficiently. 
  • 16 April 2026 SAP Business Technology Platform helps address the challenge of integrating, extending, and modernising SAP landscapes by offering a unified platform for integration, analytics, data management, and application development. For us, this means simpler system integration, better visibility through analytics, and faster innovation without disrupting our core SAP systems. 
  • 10 April 2026 By utilizing the SAP Integration Suite, we successfully automated the data flow between our core ERP and third-party tools. The biggest benefit is the dramatic reduction in manual IT maintenance, saving us countless hours each month. 
  • 16 March 2026 SAP Business Technology Platform helps us connect systems, manage data, and build extensions for SAP apps without changing the core system, keeping it stable. It speeds up development by allowing integration and new functions, ensuring easy modifications without risking the main setup. 
  • 12 February 2026  SAP technology primarily solves the problem of disconnected, inefficient, and siloed business processes within organizations. By acting as a "central nervous system," SAP integrates data across finance.         

And this is just a small sample of the reviews available on G2 – there are many more that speak about the benefits of SAP BTP not only for integration, but application development, automation and support for clean core development too.

Springing forward with new badges

Having secured our Spring 2026 badges from G2, what next for SAP BTP? Obviously, Summer 2026 badges are now in view, and with support from our fabulous customers we shall hope to achieve these next. But first for the SAP BTP team, along with others from SAP, will be a focus on our very own Sapphire 2026 events, due to take place in Orlando and Madrid next month. The SAP BTP team will be presenting at both of these prestigious events, so if you happen to be joining one or the other, please make sure to come and say hello to the team on the show floor. And if you’re unable to join either of these events in-person, then that’s no problem, instead please join us at the SAP Sapphire Virtual event.

 With that I’ll sign off for this time, but meanwhile don’t forget to go to the G2 website and read more reviews for the customer-viewpoint on SAP BTP!

Chris Grundy, Product Marketing, SAP BTP

entry.title: Triggering SAP CPI iflow through SAP Build Process Automation
entry.content:
This blog is for highlighting the basics of using SAP Build Process Automation and how it can be leveraged in various business scenarios. For demo purpose, I have created SAP CPI iflow which gets triggered by using a Automation job created in SAP BPA.

 

  1. Create CPI iflow test_iflow as below to capture data from Northwind opensource odata services with HTTPs sender adapter- 

 2. Activate the CPI iflow - 

 3. Create Destination in SAP BTP account using oauth credentials (client-id, client-secret created in BTP account) 

 

4. Add the created Destination in SAP Build->Control_Tower as below-  

 

 5. Create ‘Project’ ztestingprj_iflow under Connectors->Actions as below-  

 

 

 6.

a.  Create ‘Action’ zjoule_action under project ‘ztestingprj_iflow’ with URL [/http/ztest_joule] from endpoint generated from CPI iflow ‘test_iflow’. 

b. In the ‘Output’ tab, we will capture only some part of the response payload in the Body as the result.

c. In Destination, select the destination which was created in BTP 

d. Click Test. The Action triggers CPI iflow ‘test_iflow’ generating response [200 OK] with payload generated by CPI iflow.  

 

 7. Create ‘Process Automation’ project zProcess_prj under ‘Lobby’  

 

8. In Process Automation project created above; create Automation Artifact zauto1_sampleprocess as below- 

 

 9. In Automation Artifact created above, drag and drop the Action created in Step 6. 

 

 10. In ‘Project Properties’ create Environment Variable of type ‘destination’ as below and Save the  Automation Artifact- 

 11. To run the Automation in Desktop Agent create Automation Launcher zAutoLaunch1 as below- 

 

 12. In Automation Launcher created above drag and drop the Automation Artifact created in step 8. 

 13. Save the project. Release the required version and Deploy in ‘Public’ environment as below- 

 

 14. During Deployment, select the Destination created in BTP (step 3) in the Environment variable created in step 10.  

Click Deploy. 

 

 15. After successful Deployment the deployed version could be seen in the path as below- 

 16. Download Desktop Agent from SAP Development Tools as below and install in your system- 

 

 17. In Desktop Agent, click Add Tenant and register your SAP BPA account as below- 

 18. For Automation, set the Desktop Agent in ‘Unattended Mode’ as below- 

 

 19. After successful Tenant registration in Desktop Agent; we can see its entry in Agent Management under Control Tower path as below- 

 20. In ‘Triggers’ tab, create a trigger as per required schedule- 

 

 21. We can observe automatic triggering of the CPI iflow with the response payload- 

 22. In Monitoring->Automation Jobs; we can see the entries as per scheduled timings of trigger- 

 23. On Activating Traces, we can see the Traces showing response payloads coming from CPI iflow-

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP certified - Integration developer
entry.content:
You’ve probably worked with integrations that didn’t go as planned.  We’ve all been there.  APIs that don’t connect, flows that break, data that refuses to cooperate.

Now imagine walking into your next project with clarity.  Knowing exactly how to approach integration flows, APIs, and connectivity across SAP BTP.

That’s the goal of the Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified – Integration Developer.

In six live hours, we break down what you need for the C_CPI exam and, more importantly, what you need in real projects.  Plus, if you’ve already explored SAP Integration Suite, this is where everything starts to click.

The best part.  You don’t need to be an expert to start.  Just a solid foundation and the willingness to level up.

If certification is on your radar, this is a smart next move.

Register now and build confidence where it counts.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00009464.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #APIs #CloudIntegration #TechSkills

entry.title: Understanding Subscription Behavior in the Salesforce Adapter
entry.content:
• Why These Parameters Matter ?
  • Subscription Mechanism
  • Subscription Behaviour on Reset
  • Supported Configuration Combinations
  • Best Practices

 

With the latest enhancement to the Salesforce adapter (version 1.2.0), two powerful configuration parameters have been introduced: 

  • Subscription Mechanism
  • Subscription Behaviour on Reset  

These parameters give you fine-grained control over how events are consumed and how the adapter behaves during restarts, redeployments, or failures. This blog explains each option, when to use it, and recommended best practices. 

Why These Parameters Matter ?

In event-driven integrations with Salesforce, ensuring reliable event consumption is critical. There are several scenarios which can interrupt and thereby impact how subscriptions resume.

  • Error invoked in subscription listener  
  • iFlow redeployment  
  • Tenant restart  
  • Runtime node failure  

These new configurations help you control: 

  • Scalability (multi-node vs single-node)  
  • Data reliability (replay handling)  
  • Failure recovery behavior  

 Subscription Mechanism 

This parameter defines the number of run-time nodes that can participate in event consumption. 

      1. Subscribe on all runtime nodes 

  • All available runtime nodes create subscriptions  
  • Events Processing is distributed across nodes  
  • Designed for high throughput and scalability  

      2. Subscribe on one runtime node 

  • Only a single node actively consumes events  
  • Other nodes remain passive  

      Best for: 

  • Controlled consumption scenarios  
  • Situations where platform limits (like event consumption caps) must be managed  

      Consideration: 

  • If the active node fails, another node must take over → temporary delay possible 

Subscription Behaviour on Reset 

     This parameter defines how the adapter resumes consumption after interruption. 

      1. Replay from UI Configured Value

  • Uses the replay ID configured in the adapter UI  for all subscription creation

      Limitation:   

  • In rare cases can lead to data inconsistency during node failure.

      2. Replay from Last Processed Message 

  • Uses the last processed replay ID  for subscription creation
  • Replay id  are stored in the Integration suite persistence layer  

       Key Benefits: 

  • Ensures no data loss  
  • Automatically resumes from last processed replay id after iFlow redeployment, Tenant restart and temporary failures  

     Important Note: 
      The "Replay ID Approach" configured on the adapter configuration is only used during the first deployment. 
      After that, the  adapter always relies on the persisted replay ID.

Supported Configuration Combinations 

Let’s break down the available combinations and their behavior: 

1. Subscribe on all runtime nodes + Replay from UI Configured Value (Default) 

This is the default configuration where all runtime nodes participate in subscription.

  • All nodes consume events  
  • Replay starts from UI-defined value 

2. Subscribe on all runtime nodes + Replay from Last Processed Message (Recommended) 

This is a recommended configuration where all nodes participate in the subscription and the adapter retains the last successfully received message. In scenarios such as iFlow redeployment or tenant restarts, the adapter resumes the subscription using the stored replay ID rather than the value configured in the UI, thereby ensuring no data loss.

Note: The “Replay ID approach” configuration is applied only during initial deployment. For all subsequent subscriptions, the adapter uses the replay ID stored in the persistence layer to establish new subscriptions. To summarize, this option allows:

  • Multi-node scalability + reliable replay handling  
  • Adapter resumes from last processed message automatically  

Best choice for: 

  • If the interface is not operational in production maintenance window and want to process the replay from the last processed replay id. 
  • Where multi node capability is required for subscribing and consuming event.   

3. Subscribe on one runtime node + Replay from Last Processed Message 

This configuration is suitable for scenarios where multi-node subscription is not required. In a scenario where the Salesforce event consumption count is higher, the “Subscribe on one runtime node” option can be selected. Please note that since the subscription is active on only one node, a node failure may cause a temporary delay in event consumption until another node takes over the subscription.

This setup ensures that event consumption resumes from the last stored replay ID, once the node becomes available again after an unexpected downtime. To summarize, this option allows:

  • Single-node consumption with reliable recovery  
  • Ensures replay continuity using stored replay ID  

Note: The “Replay ID approach” configuration is applied only during the initial deployment. For all subsequent subscriptions, the adapter uses the replay ID stored in the persistence layer to establish new subscriptions.

Suitable for: 

  • High event consumption environments with platform limits  
  • Controlled processing with data safety  

 Note: Node failure may cause temporary delay, but no data loss  

 4. Subscribe on one runtime node + Replay from UI Configured Value (Least Preferred) 

This is the least preferred configuration and should only be used when you need to process events from a specific replay ID, rather than using the value stored in the persistence layer. As the subscription is active on a single node, a node failure may lead to potential event loss, since the replay ID is not persisted. To summarize, this option allows:

  • Single-node + no persisted replay tracking  
  • Subscription always depends on UI configuration  

Use only when: 

  • You must process events from a specific replay ID  

Risks: 

  • Node failure can lead to data inconsistency 

Best Practices

  • Use “Replay from Last Processed Message” wherever possible: This option ensures continuity and prevents data loss. 
  • Prefer multi-node subscription for scalability: Multi-node subscription allows for improved throughput and resilience. 
  • Use single-node subscription only when necessary: This option carries platform-imposed consumption limits and may act as a single point of failure within the system.
  • Avoid UI-based replay together with single-node : This should only be used when you need to process events from a specific replay ID, rather than using the value stored in the persistence layer.

Final Thoughts 

These new configuration options significantly enhance the flexibility of Salesforce event consumption in SAP Integration Suite. 

By choosing the right combination, you can balance: 

  • Performance  
  • Reliability  
  • Operational control  

If configured correctly, they ensure your integration remains resilient, scalable, and loss-free, even during unexpected disruptions.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite API Management Meets Agentic AI
entry.content:
Learn how our MCP Server turns SAP Integration Suite API Management into an autonomous, self-operating platform - from API creation to developer portal publication.

For over a decade, enterprise API management including in SAP Integration Suite has followed a familiar pattern. Requirements arrive, architects design the API, and developers create API proxies, configure endpoints, and apply security and traffic policies. The API is then deployed, published to the developer portal, and documented for consumption. While accelerators such as templates, reusable policies, and CI/CD pipelines streamline parts of this workflow, the lifecycle still depends heavily on human intervention at each stage.

We asked a different question: what if the platform could execute itself?

The answer is our MCP Server for SAP Integration Suite. Connect any MCP-compatible AI client -  Claude Desktop, Cursor, VS Code agents, or a custom-built agent, and SAP Integration Suite API Management becomes an autonomous, self-operating system. It interprets your intent, plans a sequence of actions, executes them, and corrects itself when something goes wrong while deploying. No scripts. No manual clicks. No back-and-forth between a developer and a UI.

What Is MCP - And Why Does It Matter Here?

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI models connect to external tools through a standardised interface. An MCP Server exposes callable tools. Any compatible client can discover and invoke them through natural language.

Our MCP Server exposes the operational depth of SAP Integration Suite API Management as AI-callable tools. The user describes an outcome. The agent does the work.

Autonomous API Lifecycle - End to End

SAP Integration Suite already covers the complete API lifecycle. Our MCP Server handsover that lifecycle to an AI agent. Here is what a single natural language instruction triggers:

API Creation

The agent selects between API as URL (lightweight passthrough), API as Proxy (fully governed intermediary), or API with API Provider (backend configuration with path mapping and version management). It reasons about what the user described and constructs the correct combination automatically.

API Policy Generation

Policies are not selected from a fixed menu. The agent reasons about your security and governance requirements based on the natural language input and constructs the right combination from the four policy groups – security policies, traffic management policies, mediation policies and extension policies.

Deployment to the API Gateway

The agent creates the API with desired API policies, sets deployment targets based on the provided input, creates API product and associates the product with the proxy, and activates the runtime, sequencing each operation correctly. If any step fails before deployment, it detects the failure, diagnoses the cause, and retries with a corrected configuration autonomously.

Developer Portal Publication

This is not a manual step left for later. The agent publishes to SAP API Business Hub as part of the same workflow. The API is discoverable by consumers the moment the agentic operation completes with no human handoff required.

Documentation Generation

Structured documentation covering endpoints, request and response schemas, authentication requirements, and usage policies is generated from the API specification and policy configuration, then published alongside the API. Documentation is not an afterthought, it is part of the agentic workflow.

The Self-Correcting Loop

The agent reviews its own work. It checks whether the API is active and reachable, policies are correctly enforced, the proxy returns expected responses, and the portal entry is live. Misconfigurations are diagnosed and corrected autonomously in the same operational cycle before deployment.

What This Means for SAP Customers

The practical implications are significant across four dimensions:

⚡  Speed

Hours of experienced consultant time - creation, policy, deployment, publishing to portal, documentation - compressed into minutes from a few natural language instructions.

■  Consistency

Policy application, naming conventions, documentation structure, portal publication, the agent handles all of it the same way on every single API.

↑  Scale

Teams bottlenecked on API management capacity now operate at a fundamentally different scale. Human experts focus on strategy and architecture decisions that genuinely require human judgement.

↺  Self-Healing

Deployment and publication failures resolved autonomously in the same cycle. The agent finds the problem, corrects it, and confirms resolution.

Connecting Your MCP Client

Any MCP-compatible client can connect. For Claude Desktop, add the server to your MCP settings with your SAP Integration Suite tenant credentials - that is it. The client discovers the full tool manifest automatically. Cursor and VS Code agents follow the same pattern. Custom agents built on any MCP-compatible framework can introspect the tool manifest and call tools directly.

Supported Clients

Claude Desktop  ·  Cursor  ·  VS Code Agent  ·  Custom MCP Agents  ·  Any MCP-compatible framework

Security and Governance — How the MCP Server Is Safeguarded

A natural question from any enterprise team evaluating agentic AI is: how is this secured? What flows through the MCP client, and how is the MCP Server itself protected? The answers are concrete and technically enforceable.

MCP Server built-in authentication

The MCP Server itself validates the connecting client using Basic Auth or Oauth 2.0. This is the first gate - an unauthenticated client cannot see what tool sever exposes.

What travels through the MCP client conversation

When a user issues a natural language instructions, the MCP client sends that instruction to the MCP Server after authentication. The MCP Server then issues instructions to SAP Integration Suite based on the user inputs. No actual business data flows through this channel. No order records, no customer data, no backend payload contents pass through the conversation. What the user describes is intent - configuration instructions, not sensitive data.

SAP Integration Suite credentials

The credentials the MCP Server uses to authenticate against SAP Integration Suite - tenant URL, client ID, and client secret are stored in the server configuration, not passed through the conversation. They never appear in the MCP client chat. Access to the MCP Server configuration should be governed with the same controls applied to SAP Integration Suite administrative access.

How the API proxies themselves are protected

Every API proxy the agent creates is secured by the policies the agent applies - OAuth 2.0, API key verification, JWT validation, IP allowlisting, quota enforcement, and threat protection. These are the same policy controls a senior integration developer would configure manually, applied consistently on every API, without exception.

Interested in understanding more. Reach out.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition seamless integration with Atlassian JIRA - Complete Ultimate Guide
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition seamless integration with Atlassian JIRA - The Ultimate Guide to the Jira Accelerator

Tired of Juggling Data Between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and Atlassian JIRA? We’ve Got You Covered!

Managing projects across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and Jira Cloud has historically meant manual data entry, misaligned project structures, and time-recording discrepancies. SAP's Jira Integration Accelerator — delivered as a pre-built integration package on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub — eliminates exactly this friction. This blog walks you through every configuration step needed to get the integration up and running in your landscape — from system prerequisites to deploying and testing each Integration Flow in SAP Integration Suite.

In the modern agile enterprise, we often see a "tug-of-war" between two critical systems: the System of Project Execution (Jira Cloud) and the System of Record (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition). When these live in silos, the result is predictable: manual data entry, financial discrepancies, and a total lack of real-time visibility.

To bridge this gap, SAP has developed and delivered the Jira Integration Accelerator available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This guide explores the architecture, data flows, and technical "Quick Facts" you need to build a seamless replication journey while maintaining a Clean Core.

The Integration package supports the following business processes:

  • Replicate Projects from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira Cloud
  • Replicate Project Team Members from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira Cloud
  • Replicate WBS Elements from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira Cloud
  • Replicate Time Recordings from Jira Cloud to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Personas:

  • Project Manager - Project Managers are responsible for delivery tracking, execution oversight, and resource coordination. They rely on Jira issues being aligned with current SAP project structures and WBS assignments so they can monitor progress, identify risks early, and maintain confidence in project execution data.
  • Finance / Cost Controller : Finance and Cost Controllers are responsible for ensuring that project-related effort and activities are assigned to the correct cost objects. They depend on accurate WBS-to-Jira mappings to support cost transparency, financial control, and reliable reporting across projects.
  • PMO / Delivery Leads : PMO and Delivery Leads are responsible for governance, reporting quality, and delivery oversight across multiple initiatives. They need visibility into inconsistencies between SAP and Jira in order to reduce manual follow-up, improve reporting accuracy, and support better execution decisions.

Business Benefits: Streamlined and automated Project Management processes over numerous touch points

  • Accurate project and employee data in Jira ensures that all Jira tasks can be assigned to valid employees and WBS elements without the need for manual configuration. This alignment guarantees consistency between both systems.
  • Multiple Jira tasks can be assigned to a single WBS element (N:1 relationship), enabling flexible and detailed operational task management in Jira without creating unnecessary WBS elements in S/4. This allows the Project Manager to freely define the tasks required for project execution in Jira, while the Project Accountant maintains a streamlined and efficient WBS structure in S/4.
  • All effort is recorded directly in Jira—the system where the project team members manage and execute their assigned tasks—eliminating the need for them to switch to another tool for time tracking. The recorded hours are automatically transferred to S/4, where activity allocations are generated.
  • These allocations credit the employee’s home cost center and debit the corresponding WBS element. Project costs are calculated based on recorded hours multiplied by the applicable cost rate. Both the effort and the associated costs are reflected in S/4 project reporting, ensuring timely and accurate visibility into project costs and potential budget deviations.
  • For projects billed on a Time and Expense (T&E) basis, the recorded hours are also available for billing purposes.

Quick Facts:

  • Target Group : SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Customers and Partners
  • Positioning : Best of Breed Integration with Jira. Integration accelerator for out of the box process and data synchronization requirements with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Licensing: Standard service plan SAP Integration Suite license, supported with all SAP Business Suite package licenses. Note: SAP Integration Suite Standard service plans include standard edition, enhanced edition, premium edition, premium edition additional instance, SAP Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, Free Tier.
  • Out-of-the-box Integration Flow’s for master data & transactional data synchronization with easy customization
  • Unidirectional synchronization from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira Cloud:
    • Project & Employee data
    • WBS Elements (Work Breakdown Structure)
  • Unidirectional synchronization from Jira Cloud to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for:
    • Time Recording – New Time Recordings, Updated Time Recordings, Deleted Time Recordings

Partner play:

  • Integration Flows for bidirectional synchronization of Time Recording between Jira Cloud & SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Integration flows for integration between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and Jira Cloud
  • UI-based integration for cross-navigation
  • Analytics

In this blog, we’ll start with a high-level overview of what the JIRA integration package offers. Then, we’ll walk you through a step-by-step guide to setting up the package — so you can get your systems connected and running efficiently with minimal effort.

Note : SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration with JIRA integration package accelerator isn’t just limited to delivered business scope, its extensible by customers and partners. The package is built with extensibility in mind, so partners can enhance it further to accommodate specific business requirements or tailor it to specific industry needs.

 

SAP provides a variety of pre-built integration accelerators via the SAP Integration Suite, all accessible through the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. Among these, we’ve developed a dedicated JIRA integration accelerator that enables a seamless connection between your JIRA cloud and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and the JIRA integration adapter is available only for SAP Integration Suite Standard service plans (SAP Integration Suite standard edition, enhanced edition, premium edition, premium edition additional instance, SAP Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, Free Tier) . For more information, see SAP Note 3188446.

The  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration with Shopify integration package ensures real-time data consistency across both platforms by synchronizing key business objects such as:

  •    Projects
  •    Project Team Members
  •    WBS (Work Breakdown Structure) Elements
  •    Time Recordings

Scope of Integration between JIRA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

 

The JIRA Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud package is designed to integrate key end-to-end business processes between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and JIRA, enabling smooth, automated data flow and operational consistency across both platforms.

The following core business processes are supported out of the box:

 From SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to JIRA:

  •   Replicate Projects - Automatically sync project headers and basic details to Jira. This ensures that every high-level project defined in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition has a matching project data in Jira, maintaining a single source of truth for project naming and duration.
  •   Replicate Project Team Members - Transfer assigned personnel from the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition project team directly to the corresponding Jira project. This automates user access and ensures that only authorized team members can log time or manage issues.
  •  Replicate WBS Elements - Synchronize your Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira, typically mapping them to Epics or Features. This allows agile teams to work within the specific financial "buckets" required for corporate budgeting. 

  From JIRA to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  •  Time Recordings - Seamlessly push Jira worklogs back into SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as CATS (Cross-Application Time Sheet) entries. This enables real-time financial tracking, accurate labor costing, and streamlined billing without manual data entry.

Together, the above listed integration flows deliver a robust, automated project management and time tracking experience, reducing manual interventions and ensuring your front-end and back-end systems remain in sync.

 

 How to Set Up the “JIRA Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud  Public Edition” Package

Integrating JIRA with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is now easier than ever — thanks to the pre-built accelerator available on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This step-by-step guide will help you configure and deploy the integration package using SAP Integration Suite, so your systems can work together seamlessly.

 Prerequisites : Before you begin, ensure you have the following:

  •  An SAP Partner Account with access to SAP Integration Suite (via SAP BTP – Business Technology Platform).
  • 🧾  Access to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
  •  Access to Atlassian JIRA cloud.
  •   Basic knowledge of SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), Integration Suite and JIRA cloud. 
  •   Access to SAP Business Accelerator Hub.
  •  Detailed Configure Guide - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration with JIRA Configuration Guide in SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Step 1. Before You Begin — System Access & Authorizations

Before configuring any Integration Flow, confirm you have the required access and authorizations across all three systems.

1.1  System Access Required

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition tenant
  • SAP Integration Suite tenant (Standard or Enterprise Edition)
  • Atlassian Jira Cloud instance with Admin access

1.2  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition — Communication Arrangements

A Technical Communication User must be created and assigned to the following three Communication Arrangements. These are mandatory — without them, the Integration Flows cannot call the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition APIs.

 

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP_COM_0027

Workforce Timesheet Integration — required for Time Recording replication (Jira → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

SAP_COM_0308

Enterprise Project Integration — required for Project and WBS Element replication (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira)

SAP_COM_0008

Business Partner, Customer and Supplier Integration — required for Employee/Team Member sync

For step-by-step instructions on creating Communication Systems, Communication Users, and Communication Arrangements, refer to the SAP Help Portal: help.sap.com → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition  → Communication Management.

1.3  SAP Integration Suite — Role Collections

The user configuring and deploying the Integration Flows must have the following Role Collections assigned in SAP BTP:

  • PI_Integration_Developer
  • PI_Business_Expert
  • PI_Administrator

1.4  Jira Cloud — Adapter Installation

Installing the official Jira Adapter is a mandatory prerequisite before any Integration Flow can be configured or deployed.

 

Action Required: Install the Jira Adapter for SAP Integration Suite before proceeding. Refer to the official 'Jira Adapter for SAP Integration Suite' setup guide on the SAP Help Portal for installation instructions.

Step 2. Configuration in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

This section covers the mandatory configurations required in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition tenant before touching SAP Integration Suite.

2.1  Create the Technical Communication User

A dedicated Technical Communication User is required for the Integration Suite to call OData APIs in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. This user handles all inbound communication from the Integration Suite.

  1. Navigate to the Communication Management section in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Create a new Communication System representing your SAP Integration Suite tenant.
  3. Create a Communication User and assign a secure password — you will need these credentials in the next section.
  4. Create Communication Arrangements for SAP_COM_0027, SAP_COM_0308, and SAP_COM_0008, linking each to the Communication System and User created above.

 

Important: Ensure all Project Team Members in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition have a valid email address in their Business Partner Profile. The Employee Replication flow uses the email address to look up and create matching Jira users — missing emails will cause the flow to skip those team members.

Step 3. Configuration in SAP Integration Suite

3.1  Create SAP S/4HANA Cloud Security Material

After completing the SAP S/4HANA Cloud configuration, the Communication User credentials must be stored as Security Material in SAP Integration Suite. All Integration Flows reference this credential by name.

  1. In SAP Integration Suite, navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs.
  2. Open Security Material.
  3. Click Create → User Credentials.
  4. Configure the credential with the following values and click on Deploy.

Parameter

Value / Description

Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  (use this exact alias — it is referenced across all iFlows)

Description

Credentials for connectivity to SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Type

User Credentials

User

The Communication User created in Step 2 (e.g. jira_accelerator_s4_technical_user)

Password

The password set for the Communication User

 

Tip: The credential name 's4hana_cloud_credentials' is the default alias pre-configured in all Integration Flows. If you use a different name, you must update the Credential Name parameter in every iFlow individually.

Step 4. Configuring and Deploying the Integration Flows

The Jira Accelerator package contains five Integration Flows (iFlows). They must be configured and deployed in the order shown below — each flow depends on the ones before it.

 

Parameter

Value / Description

Step 1

Find All Workers Assigned to WBS Elements  (supporting flow — no timer)

Step 2

Replicate Single Project Employees from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira

Step 3

Replicate Projects from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira

Step 4

Replicate WBS Elements from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira

Step 5

Replicate Time Records from Jira to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

4.1  Find All Workers Assigned to WBS Elements

The purpose of this integration flow is to find all team members assigned as resources to the project's WBS elements (work packages). It is only triggered by other integration flows, This is a supporting flow that is called internally by the other iFlows. It is not triggered by a timer — it runs only when invoked by another flow. Configure and deploy it first.

 

Sender (JIRA Integration Adapter)

Parameter

Value / Description

Adapter Type

ProcessDirect

Address

/findAllWorkersAssignedToWBSElements

 

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  (your tenant API URL)  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Proxy Type

Internet  (default)  — Optional

CSRF Protected

 Keep selected (default)  — Optional

Reuse Connection

 Keep selected (default)  — Optional

 

Once configured, Save and Deploy this flow before proceeding to any other iFlow.

4.2  Replicate Single Project Employees (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira)

This flow replicates Project Team Members from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira. It runs on a schedule and creates Jira users for each team member — matching by email address. The Jira User ID is then stored against the Business Partner record in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for bidirectional mapping.

 

Prerequisites

  • Deploy the 'Find All Workers Assigned to WBS Elements' flow (Step 4.1)
  • All Project Team Members must have a valid email address in their SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Business Partner Profile

Timer

Parameter

Value / Description

Schedule

Configure based on business needs (e.g. Daily, On Deployment for initial load)

Sender (ProcessDirect)

Parameter

Value / Description

Address

/replicateSingleEmployeeToJira

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Receiver — Jira Cloud

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Base URL

https://xxxxxxx.atlassian.net  — Mandatory

Jira Secure Material Alias

jiraAccessToken  (alias of your Jira API Token credential)  — Mandatory

More — Key Parameters

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Project Name

The SAP Project ID to sync employees for (e.g. TEST101)  — Mandatory

Jira Products For Employee

Comma-separated Jira product keys (e.g. jira-software)  — Mandatory

Default Initial Request Timestamp

1970-01-01T00:00:00Z  (first run fetches all; delta used thereafter)

Exception Logging

true  (default — recommended to keep enabled)

Customer Pre-Exit Flow Enabled

false  (default — set to true only if using custom pre-processing)

Customer Post-Exit Flow Enabled

false  (default — set to true only if using custom post-processing)

 

Bidirectional User Mapping: Once this flow runs successfully, the Jira User ID is stored as a Note against the Business Partner's default email address in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. To verify, navigate to the Business Partner record → Address Details → Additional Addresses tab.

4.3  Replicate Projects (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira)

This flow replicates Enterprise and Professional Services Projects from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Jira. It creates new Jira projects for each SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition project and keeps them updated on subsequent runs. The Jira Project Key is written back to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Project Description field (prefixed with 'JK:') for bidirectional traceability.

 

 

 

Prerequisites

  • Deploy the 'Find All Workers Assigned to WBS Elements' flow (Step 4.1)
  • Deploy the 'Create Jira Project Message Mapping' artifact
  • All Project Team Members must have valid email addresses in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Custom Project Filter

Optional — narrow down projects (e.g. and Project eq 'TEST101')

Receiver — Jira Cloud

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Base URL

https://xxxxxxx.atlassian.net  — Mandatory

Jira Secure Material Alias

jiraAccessToken  — Mandatory

More — Key Parameters

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Project Type Key

business / software / service_desk  — Mandatory

Jira Project Template Key

e.g. com.atlassian.jira-core-project-templates:jira-core-simplified-project-management  — Mandatory

Jira Preferred Project Role for User

Member  (default) — role assigned to team members  — Mandatory

Jira Default Project Role for User

Administrator  (fallback if preferred role unavailable)  — Mandatory

Project Manager Role Type

YP_RL_0001  (default SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition project manager role)  — Mandatory

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Last Request Date

1970-01-01T00:00:00  (initial load; auto-updates to delta thereafter)

Create Project Customer Exit Enabled

false  (default — enable only for custom project creation logic)

 

Jira Project Type Keys & Templates: The guide includes a full reference table of all default Jira Project Type Keys (business, software, service_desk) and their corresponding Template Keys. Match the Project Type Key and Template Key carefully — a mismatch will cause project creation to fail.

Customer Exit: To inject custom logic into project replication, enable 'Create Project Customer Exit Enabled' and configure the CustomerExit_Create_Project receiver. The final step of any custom logic must call the 'Create Jira Project Message Mapping' artifact.

4.4  Replicate WBS Elements (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira)

This flow replicates Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition into Jira as custom issue field options. This is what financially anchors every Jira ticket to a real SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition cost object. The behaviour differs slightly between Team-Managed and Company-Managed Jira projects — see the important note below.

 

 

Prerequisites

  • Projects must already be replicated using the Replicate Projects flow (Step 4.3)
  • Deploy the security artifacts required for the Integration Content

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Custom Project Filter

Optional — e.g. and Project eq 'TEST101'

Receiver — Jira Cloud

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Base URL

https://xxxxxxx.atlassian.net  — Mandatory

Jira Secure Material Alias

jiraAccessToken  — Mandatory

More — Key Parameters

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S4HANA Cloud Last Request Date

1970-01-01T00:00:00  (initial load; auto-updates to delta thereafter)

Log Exception Message

true  (default)

HTTP Session Reuse

onExchange  (default)

 

Team-Managed Projects — Manual Step Required: The Jira Cloud API does not currently support automated assignment of custom fields to Team-Managed projects. After this flow runs, you must manually add the WBS Elements custom field to each Team-Managed project:

  1. Go to Project Settings → Fields → Add field

  2. Search for 'WBS Elements' and select the field matching your project name

  3. Click 'Add 1 field', then open each Work Type and drag the field to the desired position on the issue screen

Company-Managed Projects: No manual steps required — the custom field is assigned to project screens automatically.

4.5  Replicate Time Records (Jira → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

This is the most impactful flow in the package — and the one developers interact with every day without ever knowing it. Every Jira worklog entry (new, updated, or deleted) is automatically synchronized to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Workforce Timesheet with the correct Activity Type and WBS Element. This is a fully change-aware, lifecycle-managed sync.

 

 

Prerequisites

  • Projects must be replicated (Step 4.3)
  • WBS Elements must be replicated (Step 4.4)
  • All relevant Jira users must be mapped to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Business Partners (Step 4.2)

Receiver — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Parameter

Value / Description

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Base URL

https://xxxxxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/  — Mandatory

Authentication

Basic  — Mandatory

Credential Name

s4hana_cloud_credentials  — Mandatory

Receiver — Jira Cloud

Parameter

Value / Description

Jira Base URL

https://xxxxxxx.atlassian.net  — Mandatory

Jira Secure Material Alias

jiraAccessToken  — Mandatory

More — Key Parameters

Parameter

Value / Description

Activity Type

T001  (default — maps Jira worklogs to this SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Activity Type; update to match your configuration)  — Optional

Working Hours Per Day

8  (default — must match 'Working hours per day' in Jira settings)  — Optional

Working Week

MON,TUE,WED,THU,FRI  (must match 'Working days per week' in Jira; adjust for 4-day week)  — Optional

SAP S4HANA Cloud Last Request Date

1970-01-01T00:00:00  (initial load; auto-updates to delta thereafter)

Custom Project Filter

Optional — narrow down which projects' time records are processed

Log Exception Message

true  (default — recommended)

 

Working Hours Alignment: The Working Hours Per Day and Working Week parameters must match exactly what is configured in your Jira Cloud instance under Time Tracking settings. A mismatch will cause time durations to be calculated incorrectly when transferred to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Workforce Timesheet.

Step 5. Monitoring & Operations

Once all five Integration Flows are deployed in SAP Integration suite, monitor them via the following:

  • SAP Integration Suite — Monitor → Integrations → Message Processing Logs: Check the status of each iFlow execution, inspect message payloads, and review error details for failed runs.
  • Exception Logging: With Exception Logging enabled (default: true), any errors are attached as message artifacts in the Integration Suite monitoring — making root cause analysis significantly faster.

5.1  Verifying the Integration End-to-End

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira (Projects): After deploying Step 4.3, trigger the flow manually and verify the project appears in Jira with the correct name, team members, and 'JK:' key appended to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Project Description.
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition → Jira (WBS Elements): After deploying Step 4.4, check that the WBS custom field appears on Jira issues with the correct options populated from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
  • Jira → SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Time Recording): Log a worklog entry against a Jira issue with a WBS Element selected. Trigger the Time Recording flow and verify the entry appears in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Workforce Timesheet with the correct Activity Type, WBS Element, and duration.
  • Update & Delete: Update then delete the Jira worklog and confirm both changes are reflected in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition — confirming the change-aware lifecycle management is working correctly.

Step 6. Extending the Integration with Customer Exits

Each Integration Flow supports Pre-Exit and Post-Exit extension points — allowing partners and customers to inject custom business logic before or after the standard processing, without modifying the core Integration Flow.

  • Customer Pre-Exit Flow: Triggered before the standard processing. Use this to modify or enrich the incoming payload before it is processed.
  • Customer Post-Exit Flow: Triggered after the standard processing. Use this for downstream notifications, additional system updates, or logging.
  • Create Project Customer Exit: A dedicated exit point specifically for project creation — allows customization of the project creation payload. The final step of any custom logic must call the 'Create Jira Project Message Mapping' artifact to ensure the message structure is correct.

To enable an exit, set the relevant 'Customer Pre/Post-Exit Flow Enabled' parameter to 'true' in the iFlow's More tab, and configure the corresponding ProcessDirect receiver address pointing to your custom flow.

Conclusion

With five Integration Flows, three Communication Arrangements, and a handful of configuration parameters, the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration with Jira Cloud is fully operational — bridging your System of Record and System of Innovation in a Clean Core-compliant, upgrade-stable way.

Developers stay in Jira. Finance gets real-time, accurate data in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Project managers see a consistent, synchronized project structure across both platforms. And the whole thing runs on SAP Integration Suite — with no custom code in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition core.

 

Ready to configure your integration?

Download the full SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Integration
with Jira Configuration Guide on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Jira Reconciliation Application: 

The "Jira Reconciliation" application is a utility application helping organizations reconcile project-related data between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and Atlassian Jira.

The application acts as a companion to the SAP S/4HANA integration package helping teams detect and address cross-system inconsistencies.

Features of Jira Reconciliation application: 

  • Orphaned Issue Detection - Identifies Jira issues linked to WBS elements that no longer exist in SAP S/4HANA, preventing effort from being associated with invalid project structures.
  • Missing WBS Assignment Detection - Flags Jira issues with no valid WBS link, ensuring every issue contributes to accurate project cost tracking.
  • Closed WBS Assignment Detection - Highlights Jira issues still linked to completed or closed WBS elements, preventing effort from being logged against inactive cost objects.
  • Inactive Employee Assignment Detection - Detects Jira issues assigned to employees who are no longer active in SAP, keeping resource data current and compliant.
  • Early Warning Visibility - Surfaces reconciliation issues before they propagate into financial reports or delivery dashboards.
  • Improved Cost Transparency - Supports accurate project cost tracking by keeping Jira and SAP S/4HANA in sync.
  • Reduced Manual Reconciliation Effort - Automates detection of cross-system inconsistencies that would otherwise require time-consuming manual comparison.
  • Faster Issue Resolution - Consolidates reconciliation findings in a single view so teams can identify and address problems quickly.

A big shout out to Maciej Fuchs, Amitha Kamath, Ingo Feucht, Christian Serafimov, Jan Patrzalek, Christoph Liebig, Laura Buesch-Kievit, Javit Gellaw, Stephan Hetzer, Achim Kraiss, for their invaluable contribution in realizing the Jira integration accelerator.

entry.title: When One Release Has to Reach Many SAP Cloud Integration Tenants
entry.content:
Sharing an internal tool we built for multi-tenant delivery orchestration — and asking whether other teams face the same problem.
If this resonates with enough people, I am open to releasing the source or making this available as a BTP SaaS — but first I want to know whether others face the same problem.

Show the Solution First

The fastest way to explain the solution is to show the interface - One view shows the overview, one view shows the fan-out release across source and target tenants, and one view validates whether a tenant is ready before the first delivery starts. Together, these screens make it clear that the solution is an operator workflow for delivery coordination, not just another technical utility.

When Delivery Becomes a Fan-Out Problem

In our landscape, one release can affect 15 production instances and more than 100 integration artifacts. Before orchestration, a multi-instance production release meant 30+ manual actions across several SAP tools, close to one hour of operator work, and no single place to see the full flow.
 
That is the point where delivery stops being a simple deploy step. It becomes a coordination problem.

Turning Scattered Steps into One Governed Flow

SAP Cloud Transport Management(TMS) already handled transport and routing. Content Agent Service(CAS) works for exporting MTAR files, i.e., automatically generating Transport Requests. SAP Cloud Integration already handled deployment and runtime operations. The gap was the operator workflow across many targets.
 
To close that gap, we built an internal orchestration layer we call Delivery Orchestrator for Cloud Integration. It coordinates request creation, route-aware target calculation, batch import, batch deploy, centralized status, approvals, notifications, and traceability in one place. It does not replace SAP tools. It makes them usable as one governed release flow.
 
For our team, that changed one multi-instance release from 30+ manual actions and about one hour into a 4-step flow that takes about five minutes. Just as importantly, approval and auditability moved into the workflow instead of being handled around it.
The clearest way to understand that change is the Delivery Flow view itself. One screen shows the source tenant, the target tenants, the transport path, and the import and deploy state together. For an operator, that is the difference between understanding a release and chasing it across multiple tools.

Bootstraper - Reliable Fan-Out Starts Before the First Import

Once fan-out delivery became easier, another weakness became impossible to ignore: tenant bootstrap. Connecting a new SAP Cloud Integration tenant still depended on destinations, service instances, authentication settings, and other BTP prerequisites being configured correctly. The hard part was not the manual setup itself. The hard part was that misconfiguration often remained invisible until the first real import or deploy failed.
 
That changes the meaning of bootstrap. In a one-to-many delivery model, bootstrap is not just onboarding. It is part of delivery readiness.
 
This is why guided tenant bootstrap and prerequisite validation became an important extension of the orchestration story for us. The goal is to inspect what is missing, make setup visible, automate what can be done safely, and surface what still requires manual governance. Instead of discovering a wrong URL, missing token, or incomplete service setup during runtime delivery, the operator can see those gaps before the first release starts.
 

A Secondary Capability: Version Compare

Bootstrap is the more important next step for us, because reliable onboarding is what makes the product safer to adopt across teams. But once many tenants are active, another operational question quickly matters as well: what is actually different across them?
 
That is where Version Compare becomes useful as a secondary capability. It gives operators a cross-tenant view of design-time and runtime artifact versions, highlights mismatches, and makes it easier to see where source and target tenants are no longer aligned. It is not the main story of this article, and it does not matter more than bootstrap. But it is still valuable because operational reliability is not only about moving releases forward. It is also about spotting drift early enough to act on it. 

 

A Question to the Community

We are sharing this pattern now because the core flow is already live and internally validated. The goal is not to announce a finished product, but to learn whether other SAP Cloud Integration teams face the same combination of fan-out delivery, bootstrap fragility, and cross-tenant drift.

If you operate across multiple tenants, I would be very interested to hear:
  • how you coordinate one release across many targets today
  • how much manual work still sits between transport and deployment
  • whether tenant bootstrap or prerequisite validation becomes a bottleneck in your landscape
  • how you currently see version drift across tenants

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition - your foundation for enterprise-wide agentic AI integration
entry.content:
Integration conversations with customers are changing fast.

It’s no longer about connecting systems.  It’s about enabling intelligent processes that can act, adapt, and scale.  That’s where things get interesting.

In this session, we’ll explore SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition and why it matters right now.  You’ll get a clear view of the problems it solves and how to guide customers from where they are today to something much more connected and AI-ready.

We’ll also share how to position it without overcomplicating the message.  Because sometimes, the hardest part is not the tech. It’s explaining the value in a way that clicks.

And yes, we’ll cover how agentic AI fits into all of this in a practical way.

If you’ve been looking for a clearer story to tell, this is a good place to start.

Register now and join us.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/apr/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013974.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #AgenticAI #CloudIntegration #SAPIntegrationSuite

entry.title: Everything you need to achieve Enterprise-Wide Integration and iPaaS Consolidation with SAP
entry.content:
1. Introduction – Why Integration Platform Sprawl Is Holding You Back

As enterprises accelerate their cloud journeys, integration has become both more critical—and more complex - than ever. At the same time, AI is transforming how businesses operate, intensifying the need for seamless, real-time connectivity across systems. Yet many organizations face a hidden barrier: integration platform sprawl.

A recent survey done by SAP and the Americas’ SAP Users’ Group (ASUG), with more than 500 respondents, reveals the scale of the challenge. The average enterprise manages around 18 applications requiring integration, with roughly 30 integration points per application. That translates to nearly 600 individual integrations per organization. Compounding the issue, 75% of respondents report using three or more integration tools simultaneously, as shown in a recent study by IDC.

The result is a fragmented and costly integration landscape that is difficult to manage, slows innovation, and increases exposure to security and compliance risks. There is, however, a clear path forward and it starts with simplifying and unifying integration. SAP Integration Suite plays a central role in enabling the unification.

2. The Reality of Integration Platform Sprawl

Most organizations did not end up in a position of integration sprawl by accident. Each integration platform in today's landscape is usually there for a good reason: a company acquisition, a business unit that wanted its own tooling, or there is an ongoing migration. In isolation, each of those decisions made sense. But the challenge is the cumulative effect of integration sprawl:

  • Fragmented visibility: When something breaks, teams need deep institutional knowledge just to know which platform to log into. Troubleshooting becomes slow and expensive.
  • Slowed innovation: Deciding which tool to use for a new requirement can turn into a cross-departmental negotiation - delaying projects before they even start.
  • Security and compliance risk: Every additional platform is another attack vector and another audit burden.
  • Higher total cost of ownership: Licensing, personnel, and operational overhead multiply with every platform - and skilled integration professionals end up managing infrastructure instead of delivering value.

These are not abstract risks. These challenges translate directly into costs, delays, and missed opportunities.

3. The Hidden Costs of Doing Nothing

The challenges above tend to compound over time. Many organizations find themselves sitting on significant "integration debt" - a backlog of undocumented, poorly understood interfaces that carry both technical and business risk.

At the same time, the integration tools many enterprises rely on today were built for a different era where batch processing, file transfers, and mostly on-premises architectures ruled the day. Today's reality looks very different:

  • Applications span on-premises environments, hyperscalers, and SaaS platforms all at once.
  • Business models change in weeks, not years.
  • AI agents are starting to interact with enterprise systems in real time and autonomously.
  • Data sovereignty regulations require precise control over where data flows.
  • Event-driven Architecture becomes more critical to enable business processes in real-time

An integration setup designed for yesterday simply cannot keep up with what the business needs today and tomorrow.

4. What Enterprise-Wide Integration Consolidation Looks Like

Consolidating all integration workloads onto a single, enterprise-grade integration platform delivers benefits across the organization:

  • Unified visibility and control: One place to monitor, troubleshoot, and manage. When something goes wrong, teams know exactly where to look.
  • Streamlined governance: A single, standardized pipeline for promoting changes to production. Governance becomes something you can actually enforce consistently across all teams.
  • Increased agility: No more indecision about which tool to use. Teams move faster and focus on building value rather than managing infrastructure.
  • Cost optimization: Fewer platforms mean lower licensing costs, leaner operations, and people freed up for more innovative work.

Murphy Oil consolidated multiple iPaaS solutions, including SAP Process Orchestration, to SAP Integration Suite. They migrated over 500 interfaces in about a year and achieved a 25% cost reduction for G&A spending. NuSkin saved $300,000 in the first year of consolidation alone, with ongoing annual savings reinvested into new initiatives. And CONA, a North American Coca-Cola bottler, cut its integration runtime costs by more than 50% while dramatically improving monitoring and error handling across its entire bottling ecosystem.

5. Why SAP Integration Suite Is the Right Platform for Consolidation

SAP Integration Suite is built on open standards and designed to meet you wherever you are in your integration maturity. No matter how complex or diverse your landscape is, SAP Integration Suite has the capabilities to connect your entire application landscape. The suite brings together process integration, API management, business-to-business (B2B) and business-to-government (B2G) connectivity, event-driven integration, data space integration (e.g. Catena-X), and support for AI agents in one unified platform. The breadth of integration capabilities matters. Whether you are connecting business applications, exposing SAP S/4HANA data through APIs, managing EDI or e-invoicing, processing real-time events, or calling external AI models like Joule or Gemini as part of an integration flow, SAP Integration Suite handles it all with its comprehensive suite.

For the sixth consecutive year, Gartner has recognized SAP as a leader in the Integration Platform as a Service Magic Quadrant - citing comprehensive coverage of integration styles, deep AI capabilities, and the breadth of content on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

With over 3,800 pre-built integration scenarios, 4,300 APIs for industry leading applications, 600 business events for event-driven use cases, and connectors for every single one of the top 500 enterprise applications, the platform gives organizations a significant head start. Benchmarking with partners and customers has shown up to five times faster go-live and up to ten times lower build cost compared to starting from scratch.

6. Third-Party Connectivity – Reaching Every Corner of Your Landscape

A key question when consolidating integration platforms is whether the system you're consolidating to can actually connect to everything - not just SAP systems, but the full range of third-party applications, cloud services, legacy infrastructure, and partner ecosystems in a real enterprise landscape. SAP Integration Suite offers over 250 connectors out of the box, covering two distinct types.

Technology adapters work at the protocol level. Examples of technology adapters are HTTP/HTTPS, SOAP, OData, SFTP, AMQP, JDBC, and many more. They are generic and reusable across a wide range of systems and applications, giving developers full control over authentication and communication patterns. Any system that exposes a standard protocol can be connected to in this way.

Application-specific adapters go further and are purpose-built for individual business applications like Salesforce, Workday, Shopify, ServiceNow, and many others. These adapters already know the target system's API structure and come with pre-configured operations. Less configuration means faster delivery, fewer errors, and more focus on the business process.

An independent analysis of the top 517 enterprise applications confirmed that SAP Integration Suite has a technology or application-specific adapter for every single one! Connectivity gaps that might justify keeping a secondary platform are, in practice, extremely rare. And, for the rare instances where a customer adapter is required, the SAP Integration Suite Adapter Development Kit (ADK) can be used to develop a customer adapter to meet your specific requirements.

When it comes to integration, connectivity is only the starting point. What organizations also need is the business logic, data mapping, exception handling, and proven patterns that turn a system connection into a reliable, production-grade integration. This is where the integration content on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub makes a real difference. The Hub hosts over 3,800 pre-built integration scenarios - fully realized integration flows that come with end-to-end process logic, data transformations, error handling, security validation, and best practices from real-world deployments. This is not an unregulated marketplace full of slop. Content is curated by SAP's engineering teams or by certified partners who are licensed to contribute and keep packages up to date. Every package comes with a component ID for direct support in the event of issues.

7. Integration Strategy – Getting the Foundation Right

Consolidating onto a single platform is not just a technical exercise. It requires a clear integration strategy. As more workloads move onto a singular platform, traffic increases, processes become more business-critical, and expectations for reliability grow. Your strategy needs to account for this from the start.

SAP's Integration Solution Advisory Methodology (ISA-M) provides exactly the structured foundation you need. It is a platform-agnostic proven framework for mapping your integration landscape, categorizing use cases, and defining the right integration styles for each scenario. It is designed to help teams move from reactive, ad-hoc integration to a deliberate strategy that scales with the business.

Complementing ISA-M, the integration assessment capability of SAP Integration Suite helps organizations take stock of their existing integration landscape. It assists in identifying what is running, what is redundant, what carries risk, and where consolidation opportunities exist. This is the essential starting point for a credible business case and a realistic migration roadmap. Together, ISA-M and the integration assessment capability give integration teams the language to have strategic conversations with business and IT leadership about how integration enables agility, AI readiness, and long-term scalability.

8. Platform Operations – Running SAP Integration Suite at Enterprise Scale

A solid strategy sets the direction, but the operational foundation needs to be equally strong. When a single platform carries the full weight of enterprise integration, it becomes mission-critical infrastructure. How you build, deploy, monitor, and govern integrations matters more than ever. The BTP Guidance Framework provides architectural and development best practices to ensure integrations are built consistently and securely from day one. These guidelines can be actively enforced through tooling, so standards are upheld as the platform grows.

For day-to-day operations, SAP Integration Suite provides built-in monitoring with real-time visibility into message flows and system health. This is extended further through SAP Cloud ALM, adding centralized monitoring, alerting, and health tracking across the full SAP landscape. This level of cross-landscape observability simply cannot be achieved when monitoring is split across multiple platforms.

SAP Integration Suite also supports CI/CD pipelines and DevOps workflows, so integration teams can manage artifacts with the same discipline applied to application development, including versioning, automated testing, and controlled deployment pipelines. This is exactly what you need when the platform is running hundreds or thousands of business-critical integrations at once.

9. Building the Business Case for Consolidation

When making the case for consolidation internally, framing matters. Positioning integration consolidation as a re-platforming project is difficult to sell. Consolidation and re-platforming by themselves do not create business value. Positioning the consolidation around the core principles that affect every IT organization: risk reduction, productivity improvements (through AI), cost reduction.

A few angles worth considering:

  • Integration debt reduction: Undocumented, poorly understood interfaces are a compliance and security risk. Consolidation is an opportunity to clean up the estate and reduce that exposure.
  • AI readiness: The agentic AI use cases that business leaders are asking about require clean data layers, purpose-built APIs, and real-time event-driven connectivity. Building that foundation now is what unlocks AI value later.
  • Governance and COE maturity: ISA-M gives integration teams a structured framework to get a clear picture of the current landscape and put the governance in place to operate at scale.
  • Hard cost savings: In many cases, the savings from shutting down redundant platforms and reallocating people are enough to justify the project on their own - as the Murphy Oil and NuSkin examples show.

The integration landscape is not getting simpler. AI agents, real-time event-driven architectures, composable ERP, and data sovereignty requirements are all adding new demands to what enterprise integration needs to deliver simplification through consolidation.

10. Join Our Webinar Series – See It in Action

Ready to go deeper? We have put together a webinar series that walks you through everything covered in this blog including live demos, real customer stories, and practical guidance from our product and solution experts. Whether you are just getting started or already planning your consolidation, there is a session for you.

 

  1. Rethinking Integration: Building a Unified Enterprise-wide Platform
  2. From Vision to Execution: Building a Scalable Integration Strategy
  3. Achieve Enterprise-grade Operations for SAP Integration Suite

11. What's Next? Here Is How to Get Started

Liked what you read? Below you will find everything you need to take the next step: from exploring the platform yourself to connecting with our team and diving into the content that is most relevant for your journey. We have classified the content into different personas so you can get started at the right place and at the right pace.

How to get started as Integration Strategist 

 

Asset Type

How is this asset useful?

Get started with the SAP Integration Suite website and learn more about iPaaS consolidation. Also, check out our Integration Roadmap to understand future capabilities and features.

New to SAP Integration Suite or just getting started? The product website gives you a solid platform overview, and the Roadmap Explorer shows you what is coming next -  so you can plan with confidence.

Whitepaper on Future-proof integration for cloud ERP  and SAP’s CIO Guide on Integration Architecture Guide

Need to bring leadership along? The Future-proof Integration whitepaper and CIO Guide give decision makers a concise, executive-level view of why modern integration architecture matters - and what to do about it.

Dive deeper with a demo video and our solution brief

See the platform in action and get the key facts in one place. Perfect for sharing with stakeholders who need a quick but concrete picture of what SAP Integration Suite can do.

Get started with the SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology website and learn more about integration strategies in this blogpost: SAP Integration Solution Advisory at a Glance

Consolidation starts with a clear strategy. ISA-M gives you the structured methodology to map your landscape, categorize use cases, and define the right integration patterns - before you start building.

Get enabled on our official Learning Journey: Getting started with SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology to lay the right foundation and start developing with following mission: Get started with Integration Suite, Integration Assesment

Ready to dive deeper on Integration Strategy? The Learning Journey gets you up to speed on ISA-M, while the mission catalog and Integration Assessment help you take stock of your existing estate and build a realistic roadmap.

 

How to get started as Architect

 

Asset Type

How is this asset useful?

Get enabled on our official BTP Guidance framework to lay the right foundation and access more than 100 SAP Learning Journeys for SAP BTP and more

Build on the right foundation from day one. The BTP Guidance framework and Learning Journeys give you the architectural principles and hands-on enablement you need to design integrations that are secure, scalable, and built to last.

Navigate to the SAP Integration Suite service entry page in the SAP Discovery Center

Your one-stop shop for service details, pricing, and hands-on missions. The Discovery Center gives you everything you need to evaluate SAP Integration Suite.

Take a look at the marketecture of SAP BTP and explore SAP BTP reference architectures in the SAP Architecture Center. Here you can also review the BTP solution diagrams for detailed scenario setup.

Stop designing from scratch. The BTP reference architectures and solution diagrams give you proven, real-world blueprints you can adapt to your own landscape - saving time and reducing architectural risk from the start.

 

How to get started as Integration Developer

 

Asset Type

How is this asset useful?
